stories.title: Naftali Credit Partners Arranges $75M Debt for Flatbush Condo Project
stories.excerpt: Naftali Credit Partners has closed on a $75-million loan to support the development of 757 Flatbush Ave., a nine-story ground-up condominium development in Brooklyn’s Flatbush neighborhood. The financing package includes a $58-million senior loan provided by Madison Realty Capital and a $17-million mezzanine loan provided by Naftali Credit Partners. Following a successful collaboration on 208 Delancey in 2020 and 24-01 Queens Plaza North in 2024, New Empire, a New York-based real estate development firm led by Bentey Zhou, has re-engaged with Naftali Credit Partners to finance 757 Flatbush Ave. The loan refinances existing land debt and funds the remaining hard and soft costs. “Brooklyn continues to be an important focus for residential development, particularly as demand remains strong for high-quality condominium projects,” said Glenn Grimaldi, CEO of Naftali Credit Partners. “At 757 Flatbush Avenue, New Empire is advancing a thoughtfully designed development that we believe is well aligned with the market.” The post Naftali Credit Partners Arranges $75M Debt for Flatbush Condo Project appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Naftali/Madison Realty close $75M debt on 757 Flatbush condo in Brooklyn
Senior ($58M) + mezzanine ($17M) structure refinances land, funds construction costs
Repeat developer New Empire signals strong condo financing appetite in Brooklyn market
stories.summary: Naftali Credit Partners and Madison Realty Capital arranged $75 million in financing for New Empire's 757 Flatbush Ave., a nine-story condo development in Brooklyn. The deal includes a $58 million senior loan and $17 million mezzanine tranche, refinancing existing land debt and funding remaining hard and soft costs. This marks the third consecutive project Naftali has financed for New Empire, underscoring persistent lender confidence in Brooklyn residential.
stories.impact: Brooklyn condo financing remains accessible despite broader market headwinds, signaling developer appetite continues for well-capitalized firms targeting the high-design condo segment. Repeat financing wins by the same developer-lender pair suggest strong market fundamentals in Flatbush and broader Brooklyn, though condo-specific risk (absorption, pricing) differs from rental multifamily and warrants separate underwriting rigor.

stories.title: Zilber Opens Roseville Multifamily with Cottage-Style Residences
stories.excerpt: Estia Roseville, a new residential community development by affiliated Zilber Ltd. entities Zilber Residential Group and Homes by Towne, is introducing a new housing option to Greater Sacramento with the debut of 209 cottage-style single-story apartment homes in Roseville. The complex recently held its grand opening with Roseville Mayor Krista Bernasconi, members of Roseville City Council, and other civic and community leaders in attendance. Located at 6350 Baseline Rd. in Roseville, Estia Roseville blends the privacy, comfort, and livability of single-family homes with the unit-sizing, amenities, and lower maintenance lifestyle of a professionally managed apartment community, according to Zilber Residential Group. “Estia Roseville represents and new and innovative in housing design that responds to how many people increasingly want to live,” said Mike King, director of residential investments for Zilber Residential Group. He continued, “Unlike traditional apartment communities made up of stacked two- and three-story walk-up buildings, Estia was intentionally designed to create a more private, home-like living experience.” The post Zilber Opens Roseville Multifamily with Cottage-Style Residences appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Zilber opens 209-unit cottage-style apartments at 6350 Baseline Rd., Roseville, California
Single-story design blends single-family privacy with multifamily amenities and management
Product innovation targets shifting resident preference away from traditional stacked buildings
stories.summary: Zilber Residential Group and Homes by Towne opened Estia Roseville, a 209-unit multifamily community featuring cottage-style, single-story floor plans in Roseville, California. The property combines the detached-home feel with professionally managed amenities, positioning it as an alternative to traditional walk-up or mid-rise apartment configurations. The grand opening was attended by city officials, signaling strong local support.
stories.impact: Cottage-style multifamily represents differentiated product gaining traction in secondary markets like Sacramento suburbs, addressing demographic demand for single-story, high-privacy formats without single-family maintenance burden. Architects and developers should track this trend as a competitive alternative to standard garden-style or mid-rise in Sunbelt/Western markets where land is available and single-family sentiment remains strong—product mix matters for lease-up speed and premium rents.

stories.title: John M. Corcoran Launches Leasing at Pawtucket Apartments
stories.excerpt: John M. Corcoran & Co. has launched leasing at Dexter Street Commons, a new construction 150-unit apartment community with ground-floor retail in the Conant Thread District of Pawtucket, RI. Built by New England Construction, the five-story property will be managed by John M. Corcoran & Co, with move-ins expected to begin in late August. “Dexter Street Commons was designed around how people want to live today, whether they’re commuting to Providence or Boston, working remotely, enjoying neighborhood dining and shopping, or simply seeking a home that supports their well-being,” said Kelli Ahearn-Lucas, Assistant Marketing Director at John M. Corcoran & Company. “From the co-working spaces and lounges to the commuter-friendly location and wellness-focused design, this community offers residents a place to truly live, work, and thrive.” Rents range from studios starting at $1,750 up to two-bedroom apartments starting at $2,650. Thirty of the units are designated as workforce housing for qualified renters. The post John M. Corcoran Launches Leasing at Pawtucket Apartments appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: John M. Corcoran launches leasing at 150-unit Dexter Street Commons, Pawtucket, Rhode Island
Rents $1,750 studio to $2,650 two-bed; 30 units workforce-designated; ground-floor retail included
Providence-Boston corridor location targets remote workers and commuters; move-ins late August 2025
stories.summary: Dexter Street Commons, a 150-unit new construction apartment community by John M. Corcoran & Co. in Pawtucket's Conant Thread District, has begun leasing with studios from $1,750 and two-bedrooms from $2,650. The property includes ground-floor retail, 30 workforce-housing units, co-working spaces, and transit proximity to both Providence and Boston commute corridors. Move-ins are scheduled for late August 2025.
stories.impact: Pawtucket infill development signals reinvestment in secondary New England markets leveraging transit access and remote-work flexibility to compete with Providence proper. The 20% workforce-housing component suggests either tax incentives (TIF/PILOT) or community development priorities that future Rhode Island developers should factor into underwriting; construction costs and ramp timing matter for comparable Rhode Island markets (Cranston, Warwick).

stories.title: Content Creation Platform Spotter Takes 17K SF at Thor’s 25 West 39th Street
stories.excerpt: Upload complete. Spotter, a platform that provides tools to help content creators post on YouTube, has signed a 17,000-square-foot lease at Thor Equities’ 25 West 39th Street in Midtown. The lease spans the entire 14th floor of the 16-story office building one block south of Bryant Park, according to Crain’s New York Business, which first reported the deal. The deal is a three-year lease with an asking rent of approximately $70 per square foot. It’s unclear whether the deal represents a relocation or an inaugural New York office for Spotter, which has its headquarters in Los Angeles, according to its website. Landlord Thor Equities was represented in the deal by Christopher Okada and Thomas Capuano from Okada & Company. Okada did not immediately respond to Commercial Observer’s request for comment. Josh Berg from Newmark, who represented the tenant on this lease, declined to comment. A spokesperson for Spotter did not immediately respond to a request for comment. Built in 1907, 25 West 39th Street served as the Engineering Society’s building until it was converted into an office in 1961. Thor Equities acquired the midblock building in 2006 for $82 million, according to property records. It’s unclear when Spotter will move into its new Midtown offices, but it will join several other office tenants at 25 west 39th Street, including retailer Movado Group and art collective Tin Drum. Amanda Schiavo can be reached at aschiavo@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Spotter (content creation platform) leases 17,000 SF at Thor Equities' 25 West 39th St., Manhattan
Three-year lease at ~$70/SF on full 14th floor; unclear if relocation or inaugural NYC office
Tech/creator-economy tenant signals ongoing Midtown South office demand despite broader CRE uncertainty
stories.summary: Spotter, a Los Angeles-based content creation platform, signed a 17,000-square-foot, three-year lease at $70/SF on the entire 14th floor of Thor Equities' 25 West 39th Street in Midtown Manhattan. The lease is one block south of Bryant Park. It is unclear whether Spotter is relocating to NYC or establishing a new office.
stories.impact: Minimal direct impact to multifamily; office leasing signals remain noisy for residential. Relevant only as a secondary indicator of tech/creator-economy job growth and talent concentration in Manhattan—if Spotter signals broader creator-economy hiring in NYC, watch for secondary office and residential demand spillover in Midtown-adjacent neighborhoods (Long Island City, Williamsburg).

stories.title: Construction Tops Out at New Indiana Police Department HQ
stories.excerpt: Skender, serving as general contractor, along with developer Garmong, has topped out construction on the new Westfield Police Department headquarters, a $35 million facility designed to support the city’s growth and public safety needs. The new headquarters is being built on an 11.7-acre site along Grand Park Boulevard, just north of 181st Street in Westfield, Indiana. The approximately 50,000-square-foot facility will replace the city’s existing Westfield Public Safety Building. Designed by architect Dewberry as a future-ready complex, the new headquarters will support current staffing needs while allowing for long-term expansion of up to 80,000 square feet, including a planned 30,000-square-foot addition over the next 15 to 20 years. Construction began in late 2025, with completion anticipated in 2027. “We’ve had a great partnership with the City of Westfield and the owner’s representative Veridus Group throughout this project,” said Brian Simons, President of Skender’s Indianapolis office. The post Construction Tops Out at New Indiana Police Department HQ appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Westfield Police Department HQ tops out $35M facility on 11.7 acres in Indiana
50,000 SF structure designed for 80,000 SF ultimate build-out; completion 2027
Infrastructure investment in Westfield signals municipal growth confidence north of Indianapolis
stories.summary: Skender and developer Garmong topped out a $35 million, 50,000-square-foot Westfield Police Department headquarters on 11.7 acres along Grand Park Boulevard in Westfield, Indiana, north of Indianapolis. The facility is designed for long-term expansion to 80,000 square feet over 15–20 years. Construction began in late 2025 and completion is expected in 2027.
stories.impact: Public infrastructure investment (police HQ) in a suburban Indy growth corridor is a demand signal—major civic facilities often precede residential demand as municipalities invest in growth-supporting infrastructure. Monitor Westfield/Indianapolis northwest submarket for secondary residential and multifamily activity; if this reflects broader municipal confidence, expect residential developer activity to follow in the 2027–2029 window.

stories.title: Changes in Retail Real Estate Nationwide Have Accelerated: Forum
stories.excerpt: Kicking off a panel on the nature of experiential retail and the future of destination developments during Commercial Observer’s recent Retail & Hospitality Forum, Nima Fazeli, senior vice president of development for the H.wood Group, made a statement that should have seemed more shocking than it was. “I don’t think you can really differentiate the different asset classes anymore,” Fazeli said of commercial real estate in general. “I think everybody in this room would agree that it’s all becoming blended into one homogeneous experience.” This simple statement, a dismantling of sorts of CRE’s conventional sector and asset class divisions, was just one at the event — which took place on June 18 at the Paramount Club at 1301 Avenue of the Americas in Midtown — indicating the sweeping changes engulfing the retail sector in 2026. After introductory remarks from Peter Brindley, head of real estate for building owner Elecor Properties (formerly Paramount Group), and the day’s emcee, Brian Pascus, a finance reporter for Commercial Observer, the event’s first panel included a discussion of curating leading retail corridors and the resurgence of Manhattan’s Upper East Side. David Koeppel, a partner at law firm Koeppel Rosen, noted that since COVID the area has developed a new sense of cool. “Post-COVID, there was somewhat of a void on the Upper East Side. People had left,” said Koeppel. “There were great values in residential real estate to be had, and those voids were filled by younger people starting families. Retailers like that. They love the foot traffic created by the residential tenants.” Rachel Abeles, senior vice president for customer and revenue growth for Bloomingdale’s, said the Upper East Side fixture has spent the last few years seeking to bring more ambition into their platform with an emphasis on digital growth. David Koeppel (from left), Rachel Abeles, Karen Bellantoni, and Jackie Totolo sit on a panel at the Commercial Observer Retail and H
bullets: Industry panelists at Retail & Hospitality Forum declare retail/office/hospitality asset classes 'blended'
Mixed-use and experiential retail reshaping CRE sector divisions and investment strategy
Reflects accelerated shift toward non-traditional development models in 2026
stories.summary: At a Commercial Observer Retail & Hospitality Forum on June 18 in Midtown Manhattan, developers and investors discussed sweeping changes in retail real estate. H.wood Group's Nima Fazeli stated that CRE asset-class distinctions are collapsing into a 'homogeneous experience,' reflecting broader industry recognition that retail, office, hospitality, and residential are converging into mixed-use, experiential formats.
stories.impact: Minimal direct impact to pure-play multifamily, but developers should recognize that ground-floor retail, mixed-use programming, and experiential amenities are no longer optional in multifamily projects—they are now table stakes for lease-up and pricing power. Retail distress and co-tenancy risk also matter more; ensure multifamily projects have resilient, diverse retail anchors (not high-street chains alone) to weather further retail-format disruption.

stories.title: Greenstone Partners Facilitates Sale of Forest Park Retail Center
stories.excerpt: Greenstone Partners arranged the sale of Circle Plaza, a 100% occupied multi-tenant retail center located in Forest Park, Illinois, for $8.41 million. The transaction was brokered by Greenstone Partner and Investment Advisor Connor Sheedy. Situated at the intersection of Harlem Avenue and Circle Avenue, Circle Plaza is an 18,486-square-foot retail center that features a diverse mix of nationally recognized and service-oriented tenants, including Dunkin’, Pep Boys, Bloc Dispensary, HEAL Wellness, and Sploot Veterinary Care. Originally developed in 1984, the retail center has nearly maintained 100% continuous occupancy throughout its history. “Circle Plaza offered investors a rare opportunity to acquire an irreplaceable infill located retail asset with a proven operating history and significant long-term stability,” said Hagues. Positioned adjacent to both the CTA Green Line Harlem/Lake Station and Metra’s Union Pacific West Line, Circle Plaza benefits from exceptional accessibility and visibility along one of the western suburbs’ most active retail corridors. The post Greenstone Partners Facilitates Sale of Forest Park Retail Center appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Circle Plaza, 18,486-SF retail center in Forest Park, Illinois, sells for $8.41 million (100% occupied)
Asset features Dunkin', Pep Boys, dispensary; adjacent to CTA Green Line and Metra transit
Strong operating history (1984 build) and infill location attract investor demand despite retail headwinds
stories.summary: Greenstone Partners brokered the sale of Circle Plaza, an 18,486-square-foot, 100% occupied retail center in Forest Park, Illinois, for $8.41 million. The property is anchored by tenants including Dunkin', Pep Boys, Bloc Dispensary, HEAL Wellness, and Sploot Veterinary Care. Its location adjacent to CTA and Metra transit stations provides visibility and accessibility along a major suburban retail corridor.
stories.impact: Retail transaction is tangential to multifamily, but the premium valuation ($8.41M for infill, transit-proximate retail) suggests strong fundamentals in western Chicago suburbs. Multifamily developers targeting suburban Chicago markets (Forest Park, adjacent Oak Park, River Forest) should factor in transit access and nearby retail strength into site selection and amenity planning; transit-oriented infill commands retail premiums and signals residential demand stability.

stories.title: Bread of Life starts another YIGBY project in Houston
stories.excerpt: One of Houston’s best-known YIGBY developers teased plans for another project. Bread of Life Incorporated, a charitable organization associated with Beyoncé’s former church in Houston, plans to start work next month on The Weirton, a seven-story, 99-unit supportive housing facility to be constructed at 3208 Austin Street, according to a project filing submitted to the state. The 82,000-square-foot building is scheduled for completion in September 2027, and the estimated cost of construction is $20 million, or about $202,000 per unit. Texas Department of Licensing and Regulation Filings are preliminary and subject to change. The Midtown development will include 3,000 square […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Bread of Life files 99-unit supportive housing (The Weirton) at 3208 Austin St., Houston Midtown
Seven-story, $20M project ($202K/unit); completion September 2027; includes 3K SF retail, 65 parking
Fifth Bread of Life project in Houston; charity development model targets underserved populations
stories.summary: Bread of Life Incorporated, a Houston-based nonprofit development organization affiliated with a former church, filed plans for The Weirton, a 99-unit supportive housing facility in Houston's Midtown at 3208 Austin Street. The seven-story, $20 million project (est. $202,000/unit) is scheduled for completion in September 2027 and includes 3,000 square feet of ground-floor commercial, dining areas, and 65 parking spaces. This marks Bread of Life's fifth Houston-area project.
stories.impact: Supportive/affordable housing development signals sustained nonprofit capital flows and tax-credit/HUD financing availability in Houston. While the Bread of Life model targets a different income tier than market-rate multifamily, its consistent execution (five projects) shows financing and development capacity exists in Houston—relevant for market-rate developers to understand the competitive landscape for land and labor, and to model longer lease-up curves if nearby workforce-housing projects compete for same tenant pools.

stories.title: Kosher Grocer Bingo Wholesale Buys $50M Dev Site in Bed-Stuy
stories.excerpt: Popular kosher grocery chain Bingo Wholesale is expanding its Brooklyn footprint into Bedford-Stuyvesant. Bingo’s holding company, Norworth Holdings, carried out a $50 million acquisition across three industrial property transactions along Warsoff Place and Walworth Street, according to property records filed Thursday. The selling entities, LFFT TIC and EFFT TIC, are tied to members of the Follman family, who owned the parcels. Abraham Follman served as the entity’s signatory. Among the transactions was the purchase of a $25 million commercial condo at 18 Warsoff Place, at the corner of Flushing Avenue. The industrial building spans 37,916 square feet and neighbors Bingo’s second $14 million acquisition at 39 Walworth Street. The 69,747-square-foot industrial building straddles both blocks between Flushing and Park avenues. An unaddressed Walworth Street property next door — the tax lot of which measures 13,613 square feet — was also included in the deal for $11 million. Additional air rights across the transactions totaled 172,796 square feet, according to PincusCo. The transaction is expected to result in Bingo’s fifth supermarket location. Its kosher offerings will serve Bed-Stuy’s growing Jewish community. Bingo’s stores in Brooklyn, Manhattan’s Inwood neighborhood, Monsey, N.Y., and Lakewood, N.J., all serve large Orthodox Jewish populations. Official site plans for the new Bed-Stuy spot, however, have not yet been disclosed by the grocer. Representatives for Bingo did not respond to a request for comment. The multi-parcel transaction has been years in the making, PincusCo reported, as now-resolved litigation within the Follman family sought to block the deal. Follman could not immediately be reached for comment. Emily Davis can be reached at edavis@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Kosher grocer Bingo Wholesale acquires $50M industrial portfolio in Bed-Stuy: 18 Warsoff Pl ($25M), 39 Walworth St ($14M), adjacent tax lot ($11M)
Three properties total 121,276 SF across Flushing/Park Ave.; industrial/retail use conversion planned
Large retail operator anchors Bed-Stuy industrial submarket; signals investor/operator confidence in neighborhood transformation
stories.summary: Bingo Wholesale's holding company, Norworth Holdings, acquired three industrial properties in Bedford-Stuyvesant, Brooklyn for $50 million across three transactions. The portfolio includes a $25 million acquisition of a 37,916-square-foot property at 18 Warsoff Place, a $14 million purchase of a 69,747-square-foot building at 39 Walworth Street, and an $11 million adjacent lot. The properties are positioned for commercial/retail conversion.
stories.impact: Large grocery retailer anchor investment signals Bed-Stuy is transitioning from industrial-only to mixed-use retail-residential corridor. This mega-grocer presence will attract residential demand and make the submarket more appealing for multifamily development—expect nearby residential projects to benefit from grocery and retail agglomeration. Monitor Bed-Stuy/Crown Heights for emerging residential development opportunities; the $50M retail investment de-risks the neighborhood narrative and will support rental demand and pricing for adjacent multifamily.

stories.title: Nashville Zoo Proving a Beast of an Opponent for Data Center
stories.excerpt: Data centers sometimes want the lion’s share of available resources. In what might be a first for a U.S. teeming with data center opposition, it’s a zoo that’s fighting back. On May 20, it was reported that DC Blox, an Atlanta-based digital infrastructure provider, intends to build a 69,000-square-foot data center at 648 Grassmere Park in Nashville’s Radnor neighborhood. If approved, the development would be built just yards from the Nashville Zoo — something the animal care facility is actively fighting against. The zoo, at 3777 Nolensville Pike, is home to over 3,700 animals, all of which the facility claims would be negatively impacted by the construction and utilization of the data center. Earlier this month, the zoo launched a petition against the building of the data center. Over Father’s Day weekend, the petition reached more than half a million signatures. “If the data center is built, it would have detrimental effects on our animals and the people who call South Nashville home,” Leah Trice, a representative for the Nashville Zoo, told Commercial Observer. “The property line of the proposed data center is only 320 feet from the breeding facilities of our top conservation species, the clouded leopard. These elusive animals are extremely sensitive to mechanical noises.” Trice added that the prolonged noise pollution caused by the proposed data center’s construction would cause health problems for the zoo’s animals, as well as introduce pollutants to the animal and human environment. “Stormwater runoff from this new construction would cause worse damage to our already fragile retention pond,” she said of the area, which already struggles with flooding. “The property’s water runoff goes directly into our watershed, potentially washing in pollutants that could harm our animals and the people who visit the zoo every day.” The zoo’s lawyers are in contact with DC Blox, Trice said. The zoo has until July 6 to stop the data center from being built. DC Blox did not im
bullets: DC Blox proposes 69,000-SF data center at 648 Grassmere Park, Nashville Radnor neighborhood
Nashville Zoo (3,700+ animals) launching petition, reached 500K+ signatures opposing development
Data center approval faces local opposition; illustrates zoning/NIMBYism risk for industrial/tech infrastructure
stories.summary: Atlanta-based digital infrastructure provider DC Blox plans to build a 69,000-square-foot data center at 648 Grassmere Park in Nashville's Radnor neighborhood, yards from the Nashville Zoo. The zoo, housing over 3,700 animals, has launched a petition against the project that has garnered over 500,000 signatures over Father's Day weekend, citing operational and environmental concerns.
stories.impact: Minimal direct multifamily impact, but the Nashville Zoo opposition illustrates a growing NIMBY trend against data center/industrial infrastructure in residential/mixed-use neighborhoods. For multifamily developers in Nashville and other growth markets, monitor zoning and entitlement risk; if data centers face local pushback, industrial/commercial zoning adjacent to residential could tighten, limiting mixed-use development feasibility and raising construction costs if servicing infrastructure (power, cooling) becomes constrained.

stories.title: Moinian Group Signs Deals With Three AI Firms at 60 Madison Avenue
stories.excerpt: The Moinian Group has been dealing with an avalanche of new leasing in Midtown over the last two weeks. The office landlord said it has signed 68,000 square feet of new leases with four new tenants — three technology firms and a communications company — at 60 Madison Avenue, with web developer Tenex Labs taking the largest slice at 25,000 square feet. The Moinian Group did not disclose the asking rent for the building or the length of the Tenex lease, but office asking rents in Midtown South averaged $86.38 per square foot in May, according to an office market report from CBRE. Tenex’s new office will span the entire 12th floor and penthouse level of the building, but it is unclear where the firm is relocating from. “60 Madison Avenue, with its prime Midtown South location directly on Madison Square Park, continues to attract innovative and fast-growing companies seeking high-quality office space in one of Manhattan’s most dynamic business districts,” Joseph Moinian, founder and CEO at the Moinian Group, said in a statement. “These leases, all signed within the last two weeks, demonstrate the strong demand for thoughtfully designed, move-in-ready spaces and build upon the momentum we have established at the property throughout 2026.” CBRE’s Gregg Rothkin, Hayden Pascal, Jared London, Taylor Walker and Keegan Schenk represented Moinian Group in all four deals, while Noel Flagg and E.N. Cutler of Newmark negotiated on behalf of Tenex. In a separate deal, Pace, a firm that develops artificial intelligence agentic workforce for insurance companies with backing from Sequoia Capital and Thrive Capital, also signed a lease for 17,500 square feet across the entire seventh floor of 60 Madison Avenue, according to the landlord. The deal marks a relocation and expansion from 734 Broadway, where Pace occupies 3,500 square feet, according to Nicholas Markel of Cresa, who represented the tenant “We’re customizing a prebuilt, they need space to accommodate their growing team
bullets: Moinian Group signs 68,000 SF of new leases at 60 Madison Avenue, Manhattan (four tenants)
Tenex Labs (web developer) anchors with 25K SF (entire 12th floor + penthouse); Midtown South ~$86/SF asking
Tech/AI tenant concentration signals ongoing Midtown South office demand despite market softness
stories.summary: The Moinian Group signed 68,000 square feet of new leases at 60 Madison Avenue in Midtown Manhattan with four new tenants, including three tech firms and one communications company. Web developer Tenex Labs leased 25,000 square feet (entire 12th floor and penthouse). Midtown South office rents averaged $86.38/SF in May 2025, per CBRE.
stories.impact: Tangential to multifamily, but ongoing Manhattan office leasing by tech/AI firms signals continued job concentration in Midtown and sustained residential demand in proximate neighborhoods (Flatiron, Kips Bay, Stuyvesant Town area). Multifamily developers targeting Manhattan should factor persistent tech/AI hiring into demand models; however, remote-work prevalence means office leasing no longer a perfect proxy for residential demand—segment tenants by role (engineering vs. corporate) to refine residential demand forecasts.

stories.title: Domus lands approval for standard-breaking Fulton Market apartments
stories.excerpt: Chicago City Council greenlit a Fulton Market development that could set a new density precedent for the area.  Previously, zoning officials sought to reject a plan for the planned high-rise to gain a DX-16 zoning designation, which is normally reserved for skyscrapers. The new development at 215 North Racine Avenue is set to total 29 stories and 347 apartment units. A nearby parcel of unused land slated to be transformed into a public park was also purchased by the Domus Real Estate Group, the project’s developer, to fulfill a condition of the city’s approval. A small delay in acquiring the […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Domus secures DX-16 zoning for 347-unit, 29-story apartment tower at 215 North Racine, Chicago Fulton Market
High-density designation typically reserved for skyscrapers; breaks Fulton Market's prior development planning constraints
Approval conditional on Domus purchasing adjacent land for public park; raises precedent risk for future density requests
stories.summary: Domus Real Estate Group won Chicago City Council approval for a 347-unit, 29-story apartment tower at 215 North Racine in Fulton Market, securing a DX-16 zoning designation typically reserved for major skyscrapers. The approval required Domus to purchase an adjacent parcel for conversion to public park. Zoning officials initially opposed the density, but the city ultimately approved it, raising concerns among neighbors that it may establish a new precedent for higher-density development in the historically planned district.
stories.impact: Landmark zoning win in Fulton Market signals Chicago willingness to densify even historically planned neighborhoods amid housing shortage. For multifamily developers in Fulton Market and surrounding Chicago submarkets (River North, West Loop), this opens upzoning precedent—proactive entitlement strategy on marginal sites could yield higher-density approval. However, the park condition adds cost and timeline risk; factor public-benefit contingencies into any Chicago Fulton Market feasibility model. Density precedent may also tighten future zoning as city responds to community backlash—move quickly if acquisition targets are at-risk.

stories.title: “Straight out of Las Vegas”: Developers complete E11even Residences in Miami
stories.excerpt: E11even Partners and PMG completed their first E11even-branded condo tower, and have recorded nearly all of the unit closings, according to The Real Deal’s analysis of property records.  The 65-story tower at 20 Northeast 11th Street in Miami, across the street from the E11even nightclub in downtown’s Park West district, was the first high-rise condo tower in Miami to sell out in the post-pandemic frenzy. The developers reserved 90 percent of units at E11even Club Hotel & Residences Miami within two months of presales in 2021. The building has 457 units.  E11even Partners is led by Marc Roberts, Michael Simkins […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: E11even Partners and PMG complete 457-unit, 65-story condo tower (20 NE 11th St., Miami downtown) with ~$375M sellout
First Miami post-pandemic condo tower to sell out (90% reserved within 2 months of 2021 presales); closings ~400 units recorded
Amenity package (casino-style lounge, rooftop bar, 22 hot tubs, luxury spa) targets ultra-high-net-worth buyers; first phase of two-tower development
stories.summary: E11even Club Hotel & Residences Miami, a 457-unit, 65-story condo tower at 20 Northeast 11th Street developed by E11even Partners and PMG, has recorded nearly all unit closings with roughly $375 million in total sales. The building was the first Miami post-pandemic condo tower to sell out, achieving 90% reservation within two months of 2021 presales. The property includes luxury amenities (after-hours lounge, day club, rooftop bar, spa, sports lounge, branded dining) and marks phase one of a two-tower development connected by sky bridge.
stories.impact: Miami luxury condo market shows continued strength despite national rate headwinds—strong sellout signals continued wealth inflows to South Florida and demand for ultra-luxury product. For Miami multifamily rental developers, observe: (1) condo pre-sales remain robust, pulling high-income cohort away from rentals; (2) ultra-amenity positioning (nightlife, luxury dining, casinos) may pressure nearby rental properties on lease-up if they compete for same demographic; (3) two-tower connected development could set precedent for density/amenity intensity in downtown Miami—monitor for policy shifts that open zoning for larger mixed-use projects.

stories.title: The challenge of cheap markets
stories.excerpt: Studios for $1,050 a month, one-bedrooms for $1,310 and two-bedrooms for $1,525. Those are actual asking rents for “high end,” downtown, market-rate units in the Erdace Apartments, “the epitome of a luxe residence.” As you might guess, they are not in New York City. They’re in downtown Lake Charles, Louisiana. These markets might as well be on different planets. A developer in Gotham would make a killing if he could build luxury rentals for $160,000 per unit, which in 2018 is what the Erdace Apartments were projected to cost. A developer building at that price in Lake Charles, by contrast, […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Muss Development and Amesbury Companies acquire 270-unit Erdace Apartments (Lake Charles, Louisiana) for ~$137K/unit
Luxury rents $1,050 studio to $1,525 two-bed in secondary market; original $160K/unit dev cost vs. $137K acquisition
Secondary-market multifamily economics highlight gap between construction cost and stabilized valuation; rent growth constrained
stories.summary: Muss Development and Amesbury Companies acquired the 270-unit Erdace Apartments in Lake Charles, Louisiana for approximately $137,000 per unit via a HUD-insured loan assumption. The property opened in June 2020 with high-end finishes (in-unit dishwashers, granite counters, 10-foot ceilings, amenity-rich clubhouse) but rents only $1,050 studio, $1,310 one-bed, and $1,525 two-bed. Original development cost was roughly $160,000/unit. Muss plans minor capital improvements and rebranding.
stories.impact: Secondary-market multifamily arbitrage opportunity illustrates rent-growth cap in lower-cost metros—developers building new in Lake Charles face rent ceilings that may not support construction costs, making acquisition of performing assets at distressed basis more attractive than new construction. For Sunbelt/secondary-market developers, this signals: (1) new-construction capital must chase strong job/population growth to achieve rent levels that support hard costs; (2) established secondary markets with saturated supply have limited upside; (3) distressed asset acquisition and minor reposition strategies outperform ground-up development. Revisit markets with weak employment trends.

stories.title: Gauging the Obama Center’s real estate ripple effects across the South Side
stories.excerpt: The opening of the Obama Presidential Center on the Juneteenth holiday marks the culmination of a project that had construction costs balloon from an initial $300 million estimate to a staggering $850 million, following a half-decade legal battle with park preservation advocates, and pandemic-related construction delays. Envisioned as a 19-acre economic engine for Chicago’s South Side, the center features a towering 225-foot museum building wrapped in concrete letters from the former two-term president’s historic Selma speech. While the center’s backers have promised it would be a long-term boon for tourism and the area’s economic infrastructure, it has simultaneously accelerated real […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Obama Presidential Center opens June 19; construction costs ballooned from $300M to $850M; sparked housing cost surge and displacement on South Side
East Woodlawn median home prices doubled 2019–2025 to $440K; vacant lot inventory collapsed; outside developers speculating with townhome construction
Anchor institution catalyzed real estate speculation, reduced affordability, sparked tenant-rights legislative battles in adjacent neighborhoods
stories.summary: The Obama Presidential Center opened on Juneteenth after construction costs nearly tripled from an initial $300 million estimate to $850 million, following a half-decade legal battle and pandemic delays. The 19-acre complex on Chicago's South Side has accelerated real estate speculation and housing-cost inflation, with East Woodlawn median home prices doubling from 2019 to 2025 ($440,000). Outside developers have acquired lots to build near-million-dollar townhomes, and the 60637 ZIP code's vacant lot inventory has collapsed, sparking intense tenant-rights and displacement debates.
stories.impact: Major anchor-institution opening (presidential library) has proven to be a double-edged sword for residential real estate: (1) tourism and economic-engine benefits will take years to materialize; (2) near-term effect is speculative land acquisition, property-tax increases, and displacement risk—multifamily developers targeting Woodlawn/Bronzeville should expect rising land costs and community resistance; (3) legislative response (tenant-rights ordinances, speculation taxes) may constrain future development feasibility; (4) project cost overruns ($850M vs. $300M budget) reflect Chicago construction cost/labor inflation relevant for any downtown South Side development. Timing of development relative to community pushback is critical—early movers (pre-backlash) may have had better land/financing access.

stories.title: Terra, BH to pay $100M for billionaire Jeff Greene’s West Palm land
stories.excerpt: David Martin’s Terra and BH Group are teaming up to build waterfront high-rise condos on a West Palm Beach site that they’re set to buy from billionaire Jeff Greene.  Greene is selling the property for about $100 million, a source told The Real Deal.  The developer duo have plans for a pair of 31-story condo towers at the 1.6-acre site at 2085 North Flagler Drive, the South Florida Business Journal reported. The land is near Northwood Village and across from the waterfront Currie Park, which is undergoing a $35 million renovation. Records show Greene’s LLC paid $1.5 million for the […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Terra and BH Group acquire 1.6-acre West Palm Beach waterfront site (2085 North Flagler Drive) from billionaire Jeff Greene for ~$100M
Greene purchased land 2014 for $1.5M (6,567% gain); planned two 31-story condo towers, 281 units, retail, 362 parking
South Florida waterfront condo market remains strong; adjacent Currie Park $35M renovation signals municipal infrastructure investment
stories.summary: David Martin's Terra and BH Group acquired a 1.6-acre waterfront site at 2085 North Flagler Drive in West Palm Beach from billionaire Jeff Greene for approximately $100 million. Greene had purchased the land in 2014 for $1.5 million, representing a 6,567% appreciation. The developers plan two 31-story condo towers with 281 units, 4,400 square feet of retail, and 362 parking spaces, with unit sizes ranging from 1,065 to 4,510 square feet. The site is near Currie Park, which is undergoing a $35 million renovation.
stories.impact: Waterfront West Palm Beach condo market remains robust—$100M land price signals strong developer conviction in South Florida luxury condo demand and financing availability. The adjacent Currie Park renovation ($35M public investment) de-risks the submarket and supports long-term appreciation trajectory. For multifamily developers in Miami/Fort Lauderdale: (1) condo market strength pulls high-income renters away—expect rental-demand pressure if nearby condo supply increases; (2) waterfront premium ($100M for 1.6 acres) reflects scarcity value—monitor land availability and price appreciation in growth corridors to time acquisitions; (3) mixed-income retail components in condo towers may compete with standalone multifamily projects on retail anchor strength.
